metadata.summary: Production observability stack for LLM systems: tracing, cost analytics, quality evaluation, and alerting via OpenTelemetry. Vendor-neutral approach for 2026.

content.title: LLM Observability Stack
content.summary: Production observability stack for LLM systems: tracing, cost analytics, quality evaluation, and alerting via OpenTelemetry. Vendor-neutral approach for 2026.

why.p: LLM systems fail in ways invisible to traditional APM: hallucinations, cost spikes, TTFT degradation, and multi-turn context corruption. A dedicated observability stack exposes token cost per conversation, per-request quality scores, and agent reasoning chains — none of which are visible in CPU/memory metrics.

list.item: Going to production with an LLM feature — set up tracing before the first real users
Diagnosing quality regressions after a model, prompt, or retrieval change
Enforcing budget controls: daily spend alerts, cost per team/feature
Debugging agentic loops with nested tool calls and multi-step reasoning
Compliance contexts requiring audit trails of every LLM interaction
Prototype/experiment phase where data volume is negligible — add a TODO for later
Purely batch offline workloads with no SLA — standard job monitoring suffices
Primary bottleneck is something other than LLM calls (DB, network) — profile those first

section.name: why-llm-observability-differs-from-traditional-apm
section.p: Traditional APM monitors infrastructure and application health. LLM systems require a different model:

list.item: Traditional: CPU/memory/uptime → LLM: token usage, cost per conversation
Traditional: error rates → LLM: hallucination rate, quality scores
Traditional: single-request latency → LLM: TTFT, streaming throughput, P50/P95/P99
Traditional: deterministic output → LLM: non-deterministic, needs LLM-as-judge evaluation
Traditional: single-request scope → LLM: multi-turn conversation tracking with context

section.name: platform-comparison-2026
section.p: The observability platform landscape for LLM systems in 2026 includes specialized tools designed for different deployment models and organizational needs:

list.item: Langfuse — open source (MIT), self-host, tracing + prompts + evals; best for privacy/compliance
LangSmith — SaaS by LangChain, agent debugging, ideal for LangChain/LangGraph users
Arize Phoenix — open source, evaluation + embeddings, good for ML teams
Helicone — proxy-based, cost analytics + caching, free 10K calls then $79/mo
Portkey — AI gateway, multi-provider routing + fallback, usage-based
OpenLLMetry — open source (Apache 2.0), 23+ backends, OTEL-native; 6.6k+ stars
Datadog LLM Obs — enterprise, full-stack APM, no self-host
Braintrust — SaaS, multi-agent tracing, enterprise pricing

rule.statement: Choose Langfuse for self-hosted production; Helicone for cost-first optimization; LangSmith only if already on LangChain.
rule.rationale: Langfuse is MIT-licensed, actively maintained, and covers tracing + evals in one tool. Helicone adds a proxy layer that tracks costs without code changes. LangSmith's value is its deep LangGraph integration; outside that stack it adds a vendor dependency for little gain.

section.name: metrics-taxonomy
section.p: A comprehensive observability strategy tracks multiple dimensions of LLM system behavior:

list.item: Cost: token usage (input/output), $/conversation, cache hit rate, cost by user/team/feature
Quality: relevance scores, hallucination rate, user feedback (thumbs), safety scores
Performance: latency P50/P95/P99, TTFT (time to first token), throughput, queue depth
Reliability: error rate, retry rate, fallback trigger rate, timeout rate
Agent-specific: tool call success rate, reasoning step count, iteration count, loop detection

section.name: opentelemetry-integration
section.p: OpenTelemetry (OTEL) is the emerging standard for vendor-neutral tracing of LLM systems. It decouples instrumentation from backend choice:

rule.statement: Instrument via OpenTelemetry and route to any backend — never use vendor SDKs as the primary tracing layer.
rule.rationale: OTEL gives vendor portability. Switching from Langfuse to Datadog requires only a config change when using OTEL exporters; it requires a full re-instrument when using vendor-specific SDKs.

list.item: OTEL Collector receives spans on port 4317 (gRPC) or 4318 (HTTP)
Route to Langfuse via otlp/langfuse exporter with Authorization header
Route to Prometheus via prometheus exporter on port 8889 for metrics
OpenLLMetry auto-instruments OpenAI, Anthropic, LangChain calls with zero code changes

section.name: stack-selection-decision-tree
section.p: Platform selection depends on deployment constraints and organizational priorities:

list.item: Need self-hosting (privacy/compliance)? → Langfuse (MIT) or Arize Phoenix
Using LangChain/LangGraph? → LangSmith
Primary goal is cost optimization? → Helicone
Multi-provider routing + fallback? → Portkey
Existing APM (Datadog, Grafana)? → OpenLLMetry OTEL bridge
General purpose, no constraints? → Langfuse Cloud

section.name: instrumentation-rules
section.p: Proper instrumentation is critical to extract signal from an LLM observability platform. Each tracing point must capture the right context:

rule.statement: Attach user_id, session_id, and feature_flag to every LLM span at call time — not post-hoc.
rule.rationale: Post-hoc enrichment requires a join on a secondary store. Inline attribution enables per-user cost breakdown, session replay, and A/B comparison directly in the tracing UI.

rule.statement: Log both input and output token counts per call; never derive cost from character count alone.
rule.rationale: Character count diverges from token count for non-ASCII content, code, and structured JSON. Token counts are available directly from provider responses and are the only reliable cost signal.

list.item: Wrap every external LLM call in a span; include model name, provider, temperature, max_tokens
For streaming responses, record TTFT (time to first token) as a separate metric
Record cache hit/miss on every call to track prompt caching effectiveness
For agents: create parent span per agent run, child spans per tool call; include iteration count

section.name: production-alert-thresholds
section.p: Alerting strategy must balance responsiveness with alert fatigue. Production baselines for LLM systems differ significantly from traditional infrastructure:

rule.statement: Alert on these baselines: error rate >5%, P99 latency >10s, TTFT P95 >2s, hallucination rate >10%, daily spend >80% of budget.
rule.rationale: These thresholds represent practical production baselines. Lower thresholds create alert fatigue; higher thresholds miss meaningful degradation. Tune to your SLA after establishing a 2-week baseline.

list.item: Cost spike: hourly spend >150% of 7-day rolling average → warning
Quality drop: average quality score <3.5/5 sustained for 15 minutes → warning
Cache hit rate <20% for 30 minutes → info (investigate prompt variation)
All critical alerts → PagerDuty; warnings → Slack #llm-alerts channel

section.name: cost-tracking-patterns
section.p: Cost management is a critical dimension of LLM operations. Structured cost tracking enables budget control and per-team accountability:

list.item: Use pricing.yaml to centralize model costs — never hardcode rates in application code
Track cost by team, environment, and feature via span attributes; bill back to owners
Budget allocation: production 70%, staging 20%, development 10% of monthly budget
Self-hosted break-even: ~30,000 minutes/month (500 GPU-hours); below that use APIs

section.name: agent-tracing-patterns
section.p: Agentic systems introduce unique tracing challenges due to multi-step reasoning and tool interactions. Hierarchical tracing is essential:

rule.statement: Create a parent trace per agent invocation and nest all tool calls as child spans — never emit flat independent spans.
rule.rationale: Flat spans make it impossible to reconstruct reasoning chains or correlate tool results with the LLM decision that triggered them. Hierarchical spans enable full session replay.

list.item: Record tool_name, input_args, output_size, and success/error for every tool call span
Log intermediate reasoning steps (thought content) as span events, not separate spans
Detect loops: flag when the same tool is called with identical args >2 times in one session
Store full conversation history in trace metadata for debugging hallucination root causes

antipattern.description: Sampling all traces at <100% in development.
antipattern.reason: Development traffic is low enough to sample at 100%. Sampling misses the exact anomalous call you need to debug. Reserve sampling strategies (e.g., 10%) for high-volume production only.

antipattern.description: Using Prometheus counters for LLM cost without per-model labels.
antipattern.reason: A single llm_cost_usd counter masks model mix. If GPT-4o spikes while GPT-4o-mini stays flat, an unlabeled counter hides the root cause. Always include model and provider labels.

antipattern.description: Only alerting on error rate, ignoring quality score drift.
antipattern.reason: Quality regressions (relevance drop, hallucination increase) do not surface as HTTP errors. They show up as user churn. Quality alerts require LLM-as-judge sampling running continuously in production.

template.purpose: Docker Compose: Langfuse + Prometheus + Grafana + OTEL Collector
Prometheus alert rules for error rate, latency, cost, quality, cache hit rate
Model pricing config (Q1 2026) for cost calculation
